caveats: a: O*NET measures an occupation rather than the industry's full wage-weighted task mix.
a: BLS occupation projections are economy-wide and reflect employment rather than current task exposure.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines different vintages and does not identify controllable labor by workflow.
rho: Gallup covers all employed U.S. adults rather than document-preparation firms or the lower-middle-market band.
rho: Office and administrative workers in Gallup report less benefit than technical and professional workers, so broad adoption does not guarantee workflow redesign.
rho: The estimate assumes usable speech, language, and document models continue to improve over five years without eliminating review obligations.
e: No source measures repeat outsourced-service eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusually high subcontractor expense, offshore labor, or owner compensation.
e: Industry boundaries can blur document preparation with translation, printing, court reporting, virtual assistance, and data processing.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
s5: The survey measures intentions, including transfers that may not satisfy the qualifying-control definition.
s5: No cited source measures completed transactions in NAICS 561410.
q: No public source measures the industry's billing-method mix or retained share of automation benefits.
q: Retention likely differs sharply between commodity transcription and high-complexity or confidential document work.
q: This range excludes implementation friction and volume loss, which are represented in rho, d5, and o.
d5: BLS industry employment is published at five-digit 56141 and is not a constant-price output measure.
d5: Occupation projections span the whole economy and may reflect insourcing, productivity, or reclassification rather than demand for outsourced firms.
d5: The current payroll series shows a sharp recent decline that may include sampling or classification volatility.
o: The evidence describes occupations and employment, not buyer substitution decisions in NAICS 561410.
o: Operator requirement varies widely between commodity text conversion and regulated, technical, or highly formatted documents.
o: This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service rather than internal labor efficiency.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers submit text, recordings, templates, or document files and pay by page, word, audio minute, project, hour, service bundle, or recurring volume commitment. Revenue ranges from one-off assignments to repeat workflow outsourcing and master-service arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal document teams, one-person or purely episodic consumer resume practices without repeat outsourced revenue, court reporting, translation, printing and copying shops, data-processing firms, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced document editing, proofreading, typing, word processing, desktop publishing, transcription, formatting, conversion, and related secretarial document services to external business, professional, nonprofit, and government customers.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes the tasks of U.S. word processors and typists, while BLS projects employment for word processors, typists, desktop publishers, proofreaders, and related office occupations. Transformation: the high-frequency tasks of typing from drafts or recordings, spelling and grammar checks, document merging, reformatting, layout adjustment, and file handling are mapped to the wage-bearing production work of NAICS 561410 firms. Direction of error: the occupation proxy may overstate exposure where firms specialize in complex, confidential, or technically exact documents, but it may understate exposure because current occupation labels do not isolate automatable coordination and quality-control subtasks.
caveats: O*NET measures an occupation rather than the industry's full wage-weighted task mix.
BLS occupation projections are economy-wide and reflect employment rather than current task exposure.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines different vintages and does not identify controllable labor by workflow.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time and payroll study from a representative sample of NAICS 561410 firms, separating transcription, editing, formatting, desktop publishing, intake, quality review, and already-automated work, would materially refine the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: The industry's core output is already digital and consists heavily of transcription, text manipulation, proofreading, formatting, and layout work that current language, speech, and document models can accelerate. Client intake, ambiguous-source resolution, technical formatting, exception handling, and final accountability remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports payroll employment for the 56141 document-preparation industry and projects steep declines for word processors and typists and a smaller decline for desktop publishers. Transformation: recent industry employment contraction and occupation outlooks are translated into a year-five constant-price demand ratio for the current document-preparation service basket, with quantity decline moderated because labor productivity and subcontracting can reduce employment faster than customer document volume. Direction of error: employment may understate service quantity when technology raises output per worker, but it may overstate durable outsourced demand if self-service and software eliminate purchases rather than only labor.
caveats: BLS industry employment is published at five-digit 56141 and is not a constant-price output measure.
Occupation projections span the whole economy and may reflect insourcing, productivity, or reclassification rather than demand for outsourced firms.
The current payroll series shows a sharp recent decline that may include sampling or classification volatility.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar series of NAICS 561410 receipts, project volumes, active customers, renewal rates, and backlog segmented by transcription, editing, formatting, desktop publishing, and document conversion would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Businesses will continue to produce reports, forms, presentations, transcripts, and other documents, but routine typing, basic proofreading, and simple layout face secular insourcing and software substitution. Recent payroll employment is lower and BLS projects continued contraction in key occupations, supporting a declining base with a wide range.

caveats: No source measures repeat outsourced-service eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusually high subcontractor expense, offshore labor, or owner compensation.
Industry boundaries can blur document preparation with translation, printing, court reporting, virtual assistance, and data processing.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using business databases, customer websites, contract records, and management interviews to tag customer mix, repeat revenue, owner dependence, service line, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is itself centered on fee-based document preparation, so most firms of sufficient scale should provide an external outsourced service. Eligibility is reduced by businesses dominated by episodic consumer resumes, owner-specific relationships, nonrepeat projects, captive-like arrangements, or operations that cannot transfer without the principal.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561410 ($2,088,403,827) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561410 ($4,209,720,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $95,834 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561410); class <5: 2,474 firms, avg revenue/firm $243,928 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $38,175 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 308 firms, avg revenue/firm $972,968 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $152,269 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 140 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,963,857 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $307,344 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,095,942 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $797,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,138,730 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,682,211 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,610,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,764,056 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 73

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET identifies the core digital manipulation, proofreading, formatting, and clerical tasks of word processors and typists, while BLS projects declining employment and attributes desktop-publisher contraction partly to task redistribution and electronic publishing. Transformation: automatable or self-serviceable routine work is removed from year-five quantity, while complex formatting, confidential handling, client-specific standards, exception resolution, and accountable quality control are retained as operator-required work. Direction of error: the occupation evidence may overstate operator need because software can absorb more end-to-end workflow, but it may understate it where buyers require security, technical precision, rapid exception handling, or contractual accountability.
caveats: The evidence describes occupations and employment, not buyer substitution decisions in NAICS 561410.
Operator requirement varies widely between commodity text conversion and regulated, technical, or highly formatted documents.
This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service rather than internal labor efficiency.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and win-loss data identifying projects canceled, internalized, shifted to software, or retained with outsourced operators, paired with error and rework data by document complexity, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Simple typing, transcription drafts, proofreading, and layout are increasingly self-serviceable. An external operator remains useful where source material is poor, formats are complex, volumes fluctuate, deadlines are tight, or customers need secure handling and defensible final quality.

caveats: No public source measures the industry's billing-method mix or retained share of automation benefits.
Retention likely differs sharply between commodity transcription and high-complexity or confidential document work.
This range excludes implementation friction and volume loss, which are represented in rho, d5, and o.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence across a representative firm sample covering billing units, fixed-fee share, minimum volumes, renewal cadence, price concessions, subcontractor pass-through, change orders, and gross margin before and after automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Per-project, per-page, per-word, and per-audio-minute pricing can preserve savings during a contract term, while faster turnaround can create capacity. Hourly work, transparent unit pricing, customer insourcing, competitive bidding, and rapid repricing of standardized services pass much of the benefit back to buyers over five years.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup measures AI use and reported workflow effects among U.S. employees, and O*NET documents that core word-processing work is performed with digital files and software. Transformation: broad adoption is adjusted upward for the technical tractability of digital document workflows but downward for client confidentiality, integration, accuracy review, legacy formats, and change management at lower-middle-market operators. Direction of error: economy-wide adoption may understate implementation in a digitally native service, while self-reported use and productivity can overstate governed production deployment and net labor savings after review.
caveats: Gallup covers all employed U.S. adults rather than document-preparation firms or the lower-middle-market band.
Office and administrative workers in Gallup report less benefit than technical and professional workers, so broad adoption does not guarantee workflow redesign.
The estimate assumes usable speech, language, and document models continue to improve over five years without eliminating review obligations.
rho.change_evidence: Operator interviews and system logs from NAICS 561410 firms showing production AI use, exception rates, review minutes, confidentiality restrictions, error claims, and realized labor hours before and after deployment would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Document intake, transcription drafts, proofreading, reformatting, and first-pass layout can be inserted into existing software workflows with limited physical-process change. Implementation should nevertheless remain below full exposure because sensitive documents, inconsistent source files, customer-specific templates, and error liability require controls and human review.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: the intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, gifts or internal transfers that are not qualifying control events, and document-preparation firms whose customer relationships or production knowledge are not transferable. Direction of error: the broad employer sample may overstate qualifying transactions, but it may understate transfer readiness for larger process-driven firms with recurring accounts.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
The survey measures intentions, including transfers that may not satisfy the qualifying-control definition.
No cited source measures completed transactions in NAICS 561410.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and eligible-firm denominators for document preparation, transcription, and desktop-publishing businesses from PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, broker interviews, and lender records would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner intentions indicate a meaningful succession pool, but intentions are not completed control deals. Transferability improves when templates, quality procedures, staff, and customer contracts are institutionalized, and deteriorates when the owner personally performs or sells the work.

principal_driver: Core production consists of digital transcription, editing, proofreading, formatting, and layout tasks that AI can materially compress.
principal_weakness: Customer self-service and rapid repricing can shrink outsourced volume and prevent labor savings from becoming durable operator cash flow.

sources.supports: Defines NAICS 561410 as letter or resume writing, editing or proofreading, typing, word processing, desktop publishing, stenography, transcription, and related secretarial document services, with relevant exclusions.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: Lists core tasks including typing from drafts and recordings, proofreading, file handling, document compilation, reformatting, layout adjustment, and scanning.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Word Processors and Typists

sources.supports: Projects word processors and typists to decline 36.1 percent, desktop publishers 12.4 percent, and provides outlooks for related office occupations.
sources.title: Occupational Projections and Worker Characteristics, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Describes desktop-publishing work and attributes projected decline partly to task redistribution and electronic rather than print publishing.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Desktop Publishers

sources.supports: Reports 2026 U.S. employee AI adoption and productivity effects while finding limited fundamental workflow transformation and weaker effects in office administrative support roles.
sources.title: Rising AI Adoption Spurs Workforce Changes

sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and documents the survey population.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports seasonally adjusted payroll employment for five-digit document preparation services, including 34.9 thousand in June 2025 and 29.8 thousand in May 2026.
sources.title: Employment and Earnings Table B-1a

title: Document Preparation Services
